title: W7 A Differentiation Multidim Spaces
body:
Mathematical Economics

     Juan Carlos Carbajal

  School of Economics UNSW–Sydney



  Week 7A    —    Term 1, 2026




                                    1/9
 Differentiation in Rn




                        2/9
               Derivatives as Linear Approximations


 Definition 1

   Let I be an open interval and f : I → R. Say that f is differentiable
   at x ∈ I if there is a linear mapping T : R → R such that
                        f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)
                  lim                                        = 0.
                  u→x              u−x

   In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x
   and denoted by T = Df (x).


We want to extend this concept to functions defined on open sets in Rn :

                        f (x) = f (x1 , x2 , . . . , xn ).




                                                                           3/9
          Derivatives in Rn as Linear Approximations



 Definition 2

  Let S be an open subset of Rn and f : S → R. Say f is differentiable
  at x ∈ S if there is a linear mapping T : Rn → R such that

                       |f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)|
                 lim                               = 0.
                u→x             ∥u − x∥

  In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x
  and denoted by T = Df (x).


Remark
 ◦ Note u → x but there is not a single direction of approximation to x.




                                                                           4/9
             The Derivative of a Linear Function



Example 1

 Let f : Rn → R be a linear function. We can represent f via a
 (column) vector a ∈ Rn such that

             f (x) = a · x = a⊤ x,   for all x ∈ Rn .




                                                                 5/9
             The Derivative of a Linear Function



Example 1

 Let f : Rn → R be a linear function. We can represent f via a
 (column) vector a ∈ Rn such that

             f (x) = a · x = a⊤ x,      for all x ∈ Rn .

 Claim Df (x) = a⊤ , for all x ∈ Rn .




                                                                 5/9
              The Derivative of a Linear Function



Example 1

 Let f : Rn → R be a linear function. We can represent f via a
 (column) vector a ∈ Rn such that

              f (x) = a · x = a⊤ x,       for all x ∈ Rn .

 Claim Df (x) = a⊤ , for all x ∈ Rn .

 Show Verify that Df (x) satisfies the definition:
        |f (u) − f (x) − T (u − x)|       |a⊤ u − a⊤ x − a⊤ (u − x)|
  lim                               = lim
 u→x             ∥u − x∥              u→x          ∥u − x∥
                                  = 0.




                                                                       5/9
           The Derivative of a Quadratic Function


Example 2

 Let g : Rn → R be defined by
                      g(x) = x⊤ x = ∥x∥2 .




                                                   6/9
           The Derivative of a Quadratic Function


Example 2

 Let g : Rn → R be defined by
                      g(x) = x⊤ x = ∥x∥2 .

 Claim Dg(x) = 2x⊤ , for all x ∈ Rn .




                                                   6/9
           The Derivative of a Quadratic Function


Example 2

 Let g : Rn → R be defined by
                      g(x) = x⊤ x = ∥x∥2 .

 Claim Dg(x) = 2x⊤ , for all x ∈ Rn .

 Show First compute
      g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x) = u⊤ u − x⊤ x − 2x⊤ (u − x)
                                = u⊤ u − 2u⊤ x + x⊤ x
                                = (u − x)⊤ (u − x) = ∥u − x∥2 .




                                                                  6/9
            The Derivative of a Quadratic Function


Example 2

 Let g : Rn → R be defined by
                         g(x) = x⊤ x = ∥x∥2 .

 Claim Dg(x) = 2x⊤ , for all x ∈ Rn .

 Show First compute
       g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x) = u⊤ u − x⊤ x − 2x⊤ (u − x)
                                  = u⊤ u − 2u⊤ x + x⊤ x
                                  = (u − x)⊤ (u − x) = ∥u − x∥2 .

 Now verify that Dg(x) satisfies the definition.
                |g(u) − g(x) − T (u − x)|       ∥u − x∥2
          lim                             = lim
         u→x            ∥u − x∥             u→x ∥u − x∥

                                            = lim ∥u − x∥ = 0.
                                                   u→x




                                                                    6/9
                       The Derivative is Unique


Suppose f is differentiable at x ∈ S ⊆ Rn .
  ◦ Denote the derivative of f at x by Df (x).

  ◦ Df (x) is represented by a 1 × n matrix (a row vector).

  ◦ The gradient vector ∇f (x) is defined as the transpose of Df (x).

  ◦ Since ∇f (x) = Df (x)⊤ , it is a column vector, thus an element of Rn .




                                                                              7/9
                       The Derivative is Unique


Suppose f is differentiable at x ∈ S ⊆ Rn .
  ◦ Denote the derivative of f at x by Df (x).

  ◦ Df (x) is represented by a 1 × n matrix (a row vector).

  ◦ The gradient vector ∇f (x) is defined as the transpose of Df (x).

  ◦ Since ∇f (x) = Df (x)⊤ , it is a column vector, thus an element of Rn .

 Proposition 1

   Let S ⊆ Rn be open. If f : S → R is differentiable at x ∈ S, then its
   derivative is unique.


Proof   Omitted.




                                                                              7/9
                      Differentiation and Continuity

 Proposition 2

    If f : S → R is differentiable at x ∈ S, there are positive real numbers
    δ1 and K such that ∥u−x∥ < δ1 implies |f (u) − f (x)| ≤ K ∥u − x∥.


Intuitively, if f is differentiable at x, then it has a bounded slope at x.




                                                                               8/9
                      Differentiation and Continuity

 Proposition 2

    If f : S → R is differentiable at x ∈ S, there are positive real numbers
    δ1 and K such that ∥u−x∥ < δ1 implies |f (u) − f (x)| ≤ K ∥u − x∥.


Intuitively, if f is differentiable at x, then it has a bounded slope at x.


 Proposition 3

    If f : S → R is differentiable at x ∈ S, the f is continuous at x.


Proof    Fix ϵ > 0 and let 0 < δ ≤ min{ Kϵ , δ1 }, where δ1 and K are as in the
above proposition. For all u ∈ S with ∥u − x∥ < δ, we have

                    |f (u) − f (x)| ≤ K ∥u − x∥
                                     < Kδ ≤ ϵ.               ■


                                                                                  8/9
                                    Examples



 Example 3

   Let S = Rn
            ++ and f : S → R be defined by


                          f (x) = xα 1 α2      αn
                                   1 x2 · · · xn .


   The derivative of f at x ∈ S is the linear map Df (x) represented by
   the gradient vector
                                                                       
                               α1
               ∇f (x) =        x1
                                  f (x), α
                                         x2
                                           2
                                             f (x), . . . , α
                                                            xn
                                                              n
                                                                f (x)       .



Of course this function is widely employed in economics. We will later use a
different method to verify that its derivative has the form given above.




                                                                                9/9
